key_points: The East India Company arrived in India as traders but gradually became rulers through military victories and political strategies.
The Battle of Plassey (1757) was won by the Company due to the betrayal of Mir Jafar and marked the start of British political power in Bengal.
The Battle of Buxar (1764) strengthened the Company's position and resulted in the grant of Diwani rights over Bengal, Bihar and Orissa.
The Company expanded its power through policies like the Subsidiary Alliance introduced by Lord Wellesley.
Later, the Doctrine of Lapse introduced by Lord Dalhousie allowed the British to annex states that had no natural heir.
examples: Mir Jafar supported the British against Siraj-ud-Daulah in the Battle of Plassey.
After the Battle of Buxar, the Mughal emperor Shah Alam II granted the Company the right to collect revenue.
Hyder Ali and Tipu Sultan of Mysore resisted British expansion.
States such as Jhansi were annexed under the Doctrine of Lapse.
formulas: British rise to power → Trade → Military victories → Revenue control → Political rule
Major battles → Plassey (1757) + Buxar (1764) = British dominance in Bengal
Expansion methods → War + Alliances + Annexation policies
exam_tips: Understand the causes and results of the Battles of Plassey and Buxar.
Remember important leaders like Robert Clive, Tipu Sultan and Lord Dalhousie.
Explain the meaning and impact of the Subsidiary Alliance and Doctrine of Lapse.
Use chronological order of events when writing long answers.
